sentences.text: June.
Well, there was-- I had a friend whose neighbor said that, why don't you come and hide in my place?
And my--
My friend's name is Alice, and she lives in Israel, Alice Brull.
And I--
B-R-U double L.
And I would not know the name of this-- but he was-- her husband was a Hungarian soldier, a captain or some sort of a-- a high-ranking in the military.
And she was alone with a child, and she said that she will give me a place to hide.
And my mother agreed to that, which was-- she felt maybe that would save me.
For me alone, yes.
And then I slept there one night.
That was in Munkacs at the-- near the marketplace.
There was this one-- one-story dwellings, very nice, very nice places, very modern.
They were the nice places in-- in the middle of the town.
And that night I went to sleep.
And that night her husband came home.
And he said-- and she told him the kitchen that there is this child here.
Well, he became so furious.
And he says, do you want to kill us?
How can you?
And he gave her a very serious talking-to.
And next morning, I went back to the ghetto.
And I was very relieved, because I really didn't want to hide.
I didn't care what's going to happen to me.
I realized as a child that this is a-- a situation where we won't come out alive.
Nothing will be good.
Nothing will be better.
We will all die.
And so, what's the difference?
And I was very relieved when I went back to the ghetto to my mother and my sister and brother.
What was the ghetto like?
Well, we lived in this friend's house that was a modern structure.
And that was-- we had a bed, beds for all of us.
And we had the food that we were able to get, whatever we brought.
We brought some food with us.
So we were rationing our food carefully.
And then all kinds of edicts would come out.
And one time, I had long hair, braids.
And at one time, there was this edict that every girl has to have her hair very short.
If they don't obey, then they're going to shave the hair off.
So my brother took me to a barber to have my hair cut.
And-- and he-- he just was so unhappy.
He-- he didn't want my hair-- He your brother?
My brother who was older than-- the next to-- to me.
And he cried so bitterly, but my head had to be cut short.
And-- and just the punishments, that if you don't do that.
In Europe, when you had long hair, that was-- was a badge that you had lovely hair and that you didn't want to cut it off.
And there was another-- every time they have thought of something else that came that we have to do.
We have to give up this.
We have to give up that.
We can't do this.
We can't do that.
We have-- and this went on for a month.
They would take people out on the market and tell them, now give us your jewelry.
They would--
This I saw happen.
And they would bludgeon people, searching them.
And if they found something, they were immediately executed.
And--
They would beat them and shoot them.
They would beat them up and then shoot them.
And if they didn't shoot them, they beat them up so that that person-- there was no way that person could survive.
And after--
Hungarian.
That-- the Germans were there.
It was Germans and Hungarians.
But the ghetto was-- in effect, we had the Germans.
They took over.
And then, of course, the Hungarians were there also, doing their-- the-- these ignorant, unbelievably ignorant, vicious people.
And the Gypsies who--who were living out in the outskirts of town in their wagons or in the huts, they now moved into the Jewish-- the homes that became vacant.
And they were living in the-- in the homes, which-- a small punishment to see that this is happening.
And the-- the name calling-- so there was hunger, punishment, attrition, insecurity, and life loss-- loss of life.
Well, when we were going from our home, and we were going, the lace curtains would part a little.
And one neighbor was-- looked sad.
Another neighbor smiled.
And they had this reaction that, well, you killed Jesus, so therefore you should suffer.
And they-- most of them were glad to be rid of the Jews.
Now, I won't say every one of them, but more-- more than not.
And that was also-- because we would-- we would deal with the Christians in business, and the foods, whatever they would sell us, the farmers and whatever.
And whenever we were involved, whatever involvement we had to have with the Christians was-- we were civilized with them.
And they showed their-- their utter hatred when this happened.
We have-- the business had to be-- eventually we lost.
We had to close them, because we were not able to get merchandise.
And-- and there was-- there was just nothing.
People did not buy luxury items.
Suitcases were for traveling, and there was not much traveling.
When the Czechs left, they bought up every bit of stock we had to pack and leave.
And we didn't replenish, couldn't get merchandise.
Well, we did-- I shouldn't say that we didn't, because that was a few years in between.
But in the end, in 1943, we no longer could get merchandise.
And they took away the license and they did not allow Jewish people to trade.
So that was lost.

But as I said, we had a supply put away that we could have lived for quite a while on that supply of flour and fat and the-- and the fruits and the vegetables which was put away, which was by then, it was the spring.
It was already depleted, but we still had enough.
And by then, whatever we could take to the ghetto, we took.
But--
We carried it.
We carried it.
No.
We put it into suitcases and we carried it.
And we-- just one trip, whatever we could.
One trip we took it.
Yes.
It was nice.
The name was--
My-- my brother Moric was in a labor camp.
He wasn't with us.
He was already.
My brother Adolf was in a labor camp.
And Helen lived in the country with her mother-in-law.
No.
She lived in a different, separate house, but her mother-in-law was in that village.
And she was in a ghetto near her village.
So she, with her two little children-- her husband was in labor camp.
So it was the mother-in-law and her two children and herself.
They were together.
I don't know the-- the name of the place where she was.
I knew it, of course.
I don't now.
And so there was-- in the ghetto was my mother, my sister Reggie, my brother Jen, and I.
We were in this, because that's what came down to.
And we had-- we had this room.
I-- the name was [?
Herman, ?] the friends.
I went to school with her.
Claudy, ?] [?
Herman ?] [?
Claudy. ?] And we were there.
And it was very interesting.
I remember that there was another family there.
They were three beautiful girls, natural blonde girls.
And-- and every once in a while, they would rip out a cream, a jar of cream, and wash their faces with the cream.
They would always cream their beautiful faces.
And I said, how can you do that?
Well, you must take care of your skin.
You can't let it go, and here, you do that too.
And-- and I said, why would anybody want to do that when-- when we are going to die?
And-- and I was-- I looked at them that they are very odd to have this-- have this hope, because basically, I am probably a pessimist, but I didn't see any hope.
And they were doing that.
And it was a very-- a very odd situation in my mind, because I-- it was only a matter of time before we will die.
One month.
And then one day we were told to line up and take whatever belongings you have.
And we had gone back to the house once, and then we took more belongings.
And now we were carrying those belongings from one end of the town to the other, where there was the brick factory, where there were the little huts for the bricks to dry.
And in-- those little huts were assigned to people, where on the way to-- to the brick factory, the Germans were with their whips, were very cruel.
And they were hitting people.
And they were-- they were just as-- like animals.
They were the animals.
And I carried so much stuff that I felt my arches were like breaking.
And I said-- I still say it-- could be wrong, but my-- my feet got injured from carrying all that weight on-- on my-- I was very thin.
I never eat much.
I didn't have a big appetite as a child.
Oh, I-- I got an appetite when it was-- there was nothing to eat anymore.
I thought I could eat by then.
But I was very skinny.
And I-- I have a feeling that my arches have-- have been injured by carrying all that stuff.
So now we get to the-- through all that unbelievable attrition, we got to the brick factory.
And there they start to punish us again with taking people out and beating them.
And girls were raped there.
My mother hid me in the-- my sister and I-- she hid us in the-- in the little hut.
And she gave me a scarf.
She put a scarf on me and wrapped up my head so that my face would hardly show.
And she stuck me in there, and don't you dare leave, because they were-- they did their-- their punitive, their attrition, their--
Their brutality that they were given reign and freedom and encouragement, which they needed very little of.
And then after a few days, we were herded into coal and cattle cars.
Germans.
Germans with the Hungarians together.
The Germans were-- they had now the orders to-- to ship these people to Auschwitz.
And if some of them are killed on the way, no problem, there's more of them.
And then we were put into the cattle cars with one bucket on each end, and no water, and no supply, no food supply.
Mother and Jen.
Yes.
And we had this-- then we were-- went on this journey.
And we-- as we went by, we were on sidings at night.
And I looked over and I saw houses where people were sitting in houses from this mountainside.
We were on the side, and there was the valley and there was a house.
And I saw the houses and-- and the-- the smoky-- the--
--windows were foggy, or whatever it was.
And I was looking at them.
Do they know what they have?
Do they understand what-- what luxury they have to be in that home, to have something to eat?
And-- and my mind was just racing with these feelings.
And then after-- we have-- on the way there, we were-- there were other wagons coming with people from the labor camps.
And now we asked them, do you know this?
Do you know my brother?
Do you know this?
And everybody was shouting questions to them.
And they said, yeah, I saw him, or yes, he died, or I don't know.
And it was going back and forth until they came around and they started to say, don't-- don't ask.
Don't do-- don't put your hands out, because they would whip.
And no water and no food was given.
And that went on, this unbelievable state.
And we were-- I think, we were about 100 people.
In that car.
At least 100 people.
Well, we were sitting.
Not laying down, but just sitting down.
It took a-- you know, some people may remember.
I don't have a memory of-- of the time span, but I would say it was a week.
No food.
And somebody got a hold of a little water and gave it to the wagons.
And then the Germans came and they chased them away.
And-- but there was a little salt we had, and they would pass around the salt to everyone.
It was-- that was the end.
And then, it was-- it was cold, and it was-- and we were trying to tell each other some things.
Whoever survives should have some information.

And my mother would give her suggestions on to my sister Reggie, who she-- she knew she was not going to survive, because she was telling-- giving her directions on life and what she should do when she is not around any longer.
My mother must have known, must have had some information on the gas chambers and-- because as the way she was preparing my sister how to handle things, she must have known.
I only know that she was telling her things, but exactly what, I could not tell you.

But to-- one day we came out of-- when we came out of the wagons, well, she was just talking when we came out of the wagons and when they opened the door in Auschwitz, she told my sister, she is your child now.
Take care of her.
Be a mother to her.
And-- and we hugged.
And she was taken to one side and we were to another side.
And that's-- she didn't tell me anything to be or to do or be, or anything.
Evidently, I-- I didn't give her any reason to don't do this or don't do that, because I was an obedient child.
So I don't think that she said, don't do this.
It wasn't ever a question of that.
But the talk that I--
It was cold in the cars, in these--
They were--
--cattle cars that you were in.
Yeah.
They were-- they had slats, which had two-inch separations.
There was two buckets, right.
And-- and we didn't have enough water, enough food, so the use of it was-- it was not constantly used, but there was still-- eventually, it was still very foul smelling.
And-- and we were getting dirtier and the-- the cars were not cleaned before.
And so whatever was there-- the use of it was made before, it was on our clothings.
So it was a very, very hard time to live in those cars for that time.
Now, I don't know how many days it took.
Yes.
And I remember her face.
They came with the-- with the dogs, the German shepherds, and the whips.
And the direct light came right at you, shone right at you with a very, very bright light.
At night.
They would have not done this in the daytime.
I have a feeling the daytime would have been less scary.
But the night, the darkness, the bright lights, the dogs the-- the yelping and the whipping of the whips and the screaming-- and-- and they didn't want anyone to be emotional.
They just didn't let you hug long enough or-- And my mother went to one side.
And I-- I remember her eyes.
I remember her eyes, how she felt, what she must have felt to leave her child, her little child, and the one who was going to take care of that little child and her little boy.
And it was hard, which is a very serious understatement.
And then we were ushered into these halls, where they took all our clothes away and they shaved our heads.
The-- there was the German--
Women.
Women.

And then we were thrown-- then we were given showers and examined for some marks, for whatever lesions that we got on the trip, because they immediately would put them into the gas chamber if there were any lesions.
And then I saw-- through a crack in the wall, I saw my brother.
And I thought I see a mirror.
Jen.
He was shaved.
And-- and then I see-- I look at his face, and I thought I saw a mirror, because him and I looked very much alike.
We were very-- we resembled each other.
It's very interesting.
It was very close and-- I look at him, and I see myself.
And we waved.
And there was it then, that he went to the men's Auschwitz, Vernichtungslager.
And we-- my sister and I went to another one.
We were in the C Lager, C.
C Lager, which was designated for destruction.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we were given just few meager-- just a something, a coat, I mean, or a dress, or whatever was happened to be thrown at us.
And then we were given-- ushered into these blocks which was they held 1,000 people.
And there was 12 people to these beds with three rows of beds.
It was two or three.
It was 3 rows of beds, 12 in each there.
And the-- in the--
Three tiers.
Bunkbeds.
Yeah.
Bunkbeds.
And the boards were not meeting.
They had a separation, so there was even more uncomfortable, because you hit the board, the edge of the board.
No mattress.
On slats, yes.
And the-- the roof was leaking.
And now we had the-- when it was leaking, we couldn't move to the side.
And we were not allowed to get off the bunk.
And then eventually, in those-- we had-- I had a sweater, some kind of a raggedy sweater.
And eventually, the clothes got lice.
And I would go to the bathroom.
There was chlor powder, chlorine powder.
I would take that chlorine powder and wash myself with cold water, because I was-- I always worried about dirt.
And it was-- it was cold water and that awfully smelling chlorine.
But we were-- we had no hair, so it didn't get into our heads.
It didn't get into our-- we had no hair on our body, but it was on the clothes.
And then the bedbugs formed in the wood from the dampness.
And that fell on us.
And we were given the mid-day-- we were given a meal when it was raining and they didn't give the food outside and they kept us in.
We had to stay in those bunks.
They would hand us in a dish with the food, which-- with soup I never ate.
I only ate the bread.
And the food, the bugs were falling into that plate.
And I-- I said, I'd rather die than eat this, because the-- the utensils they used were bedpans, whatever happened to come.
If it was a bed pan, that's what they gave.
And if it was a regular pot or whatever, that they did not worry about.
This is not for-- this is not an eating utensil.
And I said, no way am I going to eat the soup.
And I ate the bread.
And the friends took-- everybody took a spoon from the soup that I got, and they gave me a crumb of their bread.
And evidently, the bread held me, because I never ate that soup, never.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and then Mengele came around.
I-- Mengele turned me around to see if I have any pimples or lesions on my body to immediately take me for destruction.
And I-- I was not big enough or old enough to give blood.
He came in with his bloody apron and his stance of-- of power.
And he was a big man.
And-- and he put that-- that intimidating stance.
And he would yell and scream his orders.
And he would look over people, and here, this, that.
And he would turn me around to see if there's anything.
I was sent to live.
And-- and he did that very often.
He would take-- he would take an inmate and-- to the infirmary and take a blood donation.
And then one person has said, is the Jewish blood good for the German soldiers?
And he said, yes, very good.
We'll take all of yours.
And she died right on the table.
That was--
We knew her, of course, yes.
We were in this-- 1,000 people in one-- all of us from my section, from Munkacs.
These were from Munkacs and vicinity, and we knew so many of them.
And we became friendly with those, because we were talking to each other.
That was all we could do it.
And-- and they would-- if somebody dared to say whatever, they were either beaten up, or like this woman, she had her blood drained and she was thrown off bed.
And one time I said, I can't take this anymore and I-- let them kill me.
And then my sister and our friend gave me one yank on my arm.
I almost lost this arm.
They yanked me, get away, get in there.
One of the selections.
And they-- don't you dare do that.
Your sister needs you.
Don't you behave like that.
And so I realized that I'm doing it not for myself, I'm hurting my sister more.
And then we were-- we were supportive of each other as much as we could, but standing twice a day, cold morning, hot afternoon for them to count us.
We were five in a row.
And that was called zahlappell.
And after they have counted and we tallied out correctly, and it didn't work the first time, they would hand us a bread which was cut into five slices.
And then we would-- everybody would take one slice.
And I invariably fainted, because I had low blood sugar when I was hungry.
And I was weak, and I would just faint away.
But I managed to faint after or before, because if I would have fainted while they were counting, I would not be here.
So they held me from both sides, and they rubbed my neck, and they tried to keep me going.
And then in the afternoon, the same thing.
And this went on every day.
No.
We did not.
We got no number, because we were destined for destruction.
And it doesn't seem logical, why would they have wires, electric fences, and count the people so carefully when no one would be able to-- sharpshooters on the-- on the fences.
And it was very illogical, but that's what they were told to do.
Yes.
I did.
And that was-- at night I would go out to use the facilities.
And the very bright lights-- and there was somebody-- every night there was at least one person stuck to the fence.
And in the morning, they would shut off the-- the electricity and they would remove the body.
They would shoot people.
Of course, they shot people for this or-- this-- doing the-- the-- something they were not pleased with, subordination.
They would-- the gun was there and it was used.
